ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic croissant entry (FOODON_03315091). The almond and brown sugar flat variant is not individually coded; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Croissant — FoodEx2 composite pastry category; closest defensible code for a laminated wheat-flour pastry with added toppings.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for almond-topped flat croissant; A000E (composite pastry) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for this specific pastry variant; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: topped with almond slices and brown sugar caramel

serving.natural_language_portion: One flat croissant weighing approximately 80 g, as typically sold at a Singapore bakery counter.

nutrient_source.title: Almond & Brown Sugar Crisp Flat Croissant — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient panel
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this product (cr_id G00011002). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are not individually reported in the HPB panel and are set to estimated or -1 values as documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Croissant, plain — GI value 67 (analogous estimate for butter-laminated wheat-flour pastry)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for an almond and brown sugar flat croissant. The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 67 for a plain butter croissant (white wheat flour, laminated dough). This is used as the closest defensible analogous food. The brown sugar and almond topping may marginally elevate the effective GI; the estimate is therefore treated as a lower bound. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 45.8 × 0.80) / 100 = 24.6 per 80 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.26 g/100 g) exceeding the AHA solid-food sugar threshold of 10 g/100 g, combined with borderline sodium (300 mg/100 g) and low potassium (118 mg/100 g), creates an unfavourable cardiovascular mineral ratio.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this pastry; the combined sugar and sodium load with minimal potassium offset is inconsistent with blood-pressure control dietary targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (45.8 g/100 g) from T45 wheat flour with a medium-to-high GI (~67) and 11.26 g/100 g added sugar from brown sugar topping produces a high glycaemic load (GL ~24.6 per serving) that is incompatible with postprandial blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this pastry; the high glycaemic load and added sugar content will cause significant postprandial glucose excursions that undermine glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 11 g/100 g from butter lamination substantially exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold for cardiovascular concern, and the presence of trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) further elevates LDL-raising risk; cholesterol is 67 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease or elevated LDL cholesterol should avoid this pastry due to its high saturated fat and trans fat content, both of which are independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High estimated saturated fat (~11 g/100 g) from butter and refined sugar (11.26 g/100 g) promote pro-inflammatory mediators; trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory at any detectable level.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this pastry; the butter-driven saturated fat, added sugar, and trans fat content collectively promote systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Almond and Brown Sugar Crisp Flat Croissant is a Singapore bakery product consisting of a laminated wheat-flour croissant that has been physically flattened before or during baking, then generously coated with sliced almonds and finished with a brown sugar and dark sugar caramel glaze. The flattening technique maximises the surface area exposed to heat, producing an exceptionally crispy, shatteringly thin pastry with caramelised edges. The flavour profile combines the rich, buttery depth of laminated dough with the nutty fragrance of toasted almonds and the molasses-tinged sweetness of brown sugar caramel. Texturally, the product is crisp and brittle rather than soft and flaky in the conventional croissant sense. It is best consumed on the day of purchase as the caramel coating absorbs ambient moisture and loses its crispness within hours. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 406 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 21 g fat (of which approximately 11 g is saturated fat from butter), 45.8 g carbohydrate, and 11.26 g sugar. Sodium is 300 mg/100 g and dietary fibre is modest at 2.6 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 67 (medium, analogous to a plain butter croissant) with a glycaemic load of approximately 24.6 per 80 g serving. Clinically, this product is high concern for hypertension (sugar and sodium), type 2 diabetes (high glycaemic load), and cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat). It is not suitable for individuals managing any of these conditions. The product is sold in Singapore bakeries and is associated with the broader trend of Asian-style artisan pastry innovation.
common_user_queries: Calories in almond brown sugar flat croissant
Is flat croissant high in sugar?
Is crispy flat croissant suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this exact product (cr_id G00011002, confidence 0.95 in source). Saturated fat is estimated from known butter-laminated croissant composition; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdowns are not reported by HPB and are set to -1. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for a plain butter croissant.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central does not carry this exact Singapore bakery product. 2. saturated_fat_g (11.0 g/100 g) is estimated from the known saturated fat proportion of butter-laminated croissant dough (approximately 52–55% of total fat as saturated fat, consistent with USDA SR Legacy entry for plain croissant, FDC ID 174987); this is documented as an adapted value. 3. trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) is a low-end estimate consistent with commercially produced butter-laminated pastry; not directly reported by HPB. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB panel for this product. 5. All vitamin fields (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e) set to -1 with full documentation in notes: micronutrient panel not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this product. 6. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported in the HPB panel. 7. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate based on plain butter croissant from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific almond and brown sugar flat croissant variant has been published. 8. The cultural classification in the input JSON lists primary_cuisine as 'Chinese'; this has been revised to 'Singaporean bakery / Western-influenced' as the product is a Western laminated pastry sold in Singapore with Asian flavour adaptations. Multilingual naming follows the Singaporean dishes rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). 9. is_halal set to false as halal certification status is not confirmed in the source record.
